figures.gloss: The Supreme Being, God the Father, whose title translates to 'Highest Tower' or 'Highest Palace'. He reveals himself as the singular, ultimate source of all major world religions, returning to unite them into one final, universal faith.
figures.facet: He operates not as a distant prime mover, but as an active, micromanaging executive of the cosmos, directly dictating architectural blueprints, clerical promotions, and geopolitical commentary through séance.
figures.provenance: 'Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển' (Collection of Divine Messages, 1928); original 1926 séance revelations.

figures.gloss: The central object of worship, specifically the Left Eye. It represents the Yang principle, the sun, and the universal, penetrating gaze of the Creator observing the individual conscience.
figures.facet: Bypasses human anthropomorphism for a surreal, disembodied organ of surveillance and enlightenment, serving as the physical focal point that draws worshippers into the grand syncretic cathedral.
figures.provenance: Ngô Văn Chiêu's vision on Phú Quốc island (1921).

figures.gloss: The supreme feminine principle (Yin), responsible for birthing the spiritual bodies of all living beings and guiding their emotional and spiritual salvation.
figures.facet: While the Father rules the strict, bureaucratic hierarchy of the church, the Mother exists entirely outside this administrative chain of command, presiding over a separate, deeply emotional cult of maternal grace.
figures.provenance: Adapted from the Taoist Xi Wangmu; 'Kinh Thiên Đạo Và Thế Đạo' (Prayers of the Heavenly and Earthly Way).

figures.gloss: Victor Hugo, Sun Yat-sen, and Trạng Trình Nguyễn Bỉnh Khiêm. This triad—a French romantic poet, a Chinese republican revolutionary, and a Vietnamese prophet—serve as spiritual vanguards who signed the divine pact between God and Humanity.
figures.facet: Collapses the sacred and secular completely. Anti-colonial literature and democratic revolution are literally canonized as divine mechanics, elevating recent political actors to cosmic mediators.
figures.provenance: The famous mural at the entrance of the Tây Ninh Holy See; 1920s séance transcripts.

figures.gloss: The 'corbeille-à-bec', a specialized planchette held by two mediums to channel spirit-writing. It transcribes poems, decrees, and laws from the spiritual realm in French, Vietnamese, and Chinese.
figures.facet: Strips away the ecstatic, frenzied trance of traditional shamanism, replacing it with a sober, highly literate bureaucratic process. Revelation becomes a formalized dictation of institutional law.
figures.provenance: Early Caodaist spiritist sessions (1925-1926); 'Pháp Chánh Truyền' (The Religious Constitution).

figures.gloss: The theological epoch declaring that previous religions (the First and Second Amnesties) failed due to geographical isolation and human corruption, necessitating one final, unifying revelation.
figures.facet: Replaces apocalyptic judgment with an 'amnesty'. The end of the cycle is not a destructive purge, but a massive, urgent diplomatic effort by Heaven to pardon and assimilate all divided faiths.
figures.provenance: Foundational revelations to Phạ́m Công Tắc and Cao Quỳnh Cư (1926).

figures.gloss: The invisible legislative branch of the universe, inhabited by God, Buddhas, Immortals, and Saints, who formulate the divine laws executed by the earthly church.
figures.facet: Heaven is explicitly structured as a modern constitutional government. The spiritual realm acts as a legislative senate, perfectly mirroring and commanding the earthly administrative branches below it.
figures.provenance: 'Pháp Chánh Truyền' (The Religious Constitution of Caodaism).

figures.gloss: The 8th-century Chinese Tang dynasty poet, chosen by Cao Đài to serve as the 'Giáo Tông' (Spiritual Pope) of the Great Faith and primary liaison to the earthly realm.
figures.facet: God bypassed traditional ascetics and martial heroes to select an infamously drunken, rebellious romantic poet as the supreme, unyielding enforcer of universal administrative law.
figures.provenance: 'Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển'; séance records installing him as the invisible Pope.

figures.gloss: The Hộ Pháp (Defender of the Law), chief medium of the Tây Ninh séances and the faith's dominant administrative leader from the 1930s until his 1959 death in exile in Phnom Penh.
figures.facet: The bureaucrat-medium: his planchette sessions produced much of the founding canon even as his own hand built the Holy See's institutional machinery, fusing revelation and administration in one person.
figures.provenance: Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển (1928); Pháp Chánh Truyền; Victor Oliver, 'Caodai Spiritism: A Study of Religion in Vietnamese Society' (1976).

figures.gloss: A French colonial administrator who received the founding vision of the Divine Eye on Phú Quốc island in 1921; recognized within Caodaism as its first disciple.
figures.facet: He declined the offered papacy of the new church, choosing instead an ascetic, non-institutional path — the founder who refused to found an institution.
figures.provenance: Caodaist foundational accounts of the 1921 vision; Jérémy Jammes, 'Les Oracles du Cao Đài' (2014).

figures.gloss: The 'Collection of Divine Messages' (1928), the core compiled scripture of Caodaism, gathering the séance communications from Cao Đài and the higher spirits during the religion's founding years.
figures.facet: Unlike scriptures attributed to a single human author or redactor, this text names its authorship to the spirit world itself, with the mediums as mere transcribing instruments.
figures.provenance: Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển (1928 compilation); already cited as provenance across the existing caodai roster.

figures.gloss: The executive branch of Caodaism's divine government: a nine-rank clerical hierarchy headed by the Giáo Tông (Pope), administering the church on earth.
figures.facet: The earthly half of a deliberately doubled constitution — where Bát Quái Đài legislates in the invisible realm, this palace executes in the visible one, consciously modeled on the Catholic curia it seeks to supersede.
figures.provenance: Pháp Chánh Truyền (The Religious Constitution of Caodaism).

figures.gloss: The third branch of Caodaism's divine government, combining legislative-judicial and mediumistic functions; the organ through which spirit messages lawfully enter church law, headed by the Hộ Pháp.
figures.facet: The constitutional hinge itself: not a place where humans reach up to heaven, but the chartered channel through which heaven's dictation is certified down into binding earthly law.
figures.provenance: Pháp Chánh Truyền (The Religious Constitution of Caodaism).

figures.gloss: The bodhisattva of compassion (Avalokiteśvara), installed as one of the Tam Trấn (Three Governors) of the Third Amnesty, representing the Buddhist way among the era's spiritual overseers.
figures.facet: Distinct from her separate syncretism with the Jade Pool Mother's cult, here she holds a formal constitutional office — compassion made into an administrative seat.
figures.provenance: Pháp Chánh Truyền; Caodaist Tam Trấn iconography at the Tây Ninh Holy See.

figures.gloss: The apotheosized Chinese general Guan Yu, installed as one of the Tam Trấn (Three Governors), representing the Confucian way of loyalty and righteousness among the era's spiritual overseers.
figures.facet: A historical third-century soldier absorbed whole into Caodaism's constitutional pantheon, standing beside a bodhisattva and a Taoist poet-immortal as equal co-administrators of the same office.
figures.provenance: Pháp Chánh Truyền; Caodaist Tam Trấn iconography at the Tây Ninh Holy See.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme Father and Mother, ruling the Yang and Yin principles, respectively handling institutional law and emotional salvation.
Intra-tradition: The Supreme Being and his designated, physical focal point for earthly worship.
Intra-tradition: The heavenly legislative palace and its Spiritual Pope who transmits its laws to the physical church.
Intra-tradition: The Saints who deliver the divine messages and the exact mechanical instrument used to transcribe them.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly identifies its Supreme Being as the Jade Emperor/Shangdi of the Chinese pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly names Jehovah as an earlier, localized manifestation of the Supreme Being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Allah is explicitly claimed in Caodaist texts as one of the Father's prior divine identities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Holy Mother is formally defined as the absolute manifestation of the Yin principle in the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Mother's cult is heavily syncretized with Quan Am (Avalokitesvara), sharing her role of ultimate maternal compassion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Jesus Christ is explicitly incorporated into Caodaist history as the central redeeming figure of the Second Universal Amnesty.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Caodaist theological timeline fundamentally operates on adapted Buddhist kalpas (cosmic cycles) to justify its Amnesties.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The omnipresent, disembodied solar eye that observes all creation, serving as the ultimate icon of the supreme deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A final, cosmic era of renovation designed not to destroy the world, but to restore and unite a fractured humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme scribe and literary intellect who manages the administrative and legal liaison between heaven and earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Prophetic figures who consciously and explicitly attempt to stitch all prior, geographically distinct religions into a single tapestry.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Highly formalized, technical mechanisms of divination used to establish state law and construct religious architecture.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An objective, mechanical system for retrieving divine commands that removes the subjectivity of human ecstatic trance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The structured, hierarchical fullness of the divine realm, inhabited by eons/immortals radiating from the central Godhead.

GOLD SEAM: Two modern Vietnamese revelation technologies in mirror image — Đạo Mẫu brings the spirits down into ecstatic, costumed bodies; Caodaism replaced exactly that trance with sober bureaucratic spirit-writing.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme Mother presiding beside and outside the male administrative order — Caodaism's Jade Pool Mother draws on the same Vietnamese mother-goddess current that Đạo Mẫu crowns.
GOLD SEAM: Two Mekong-era announcements that the final age has arrived — Caodaism answers with a universal amnesty, Hòa Hảo with a judgment assembly.

GOLD SEAM: Mirror answers to representing the absolute in colonial Cochinchina — Caodaism builds a cathedral around a single divine Eye; Hòa Hảo hangs a plain brown cloth and calls the household a temple.
Revelation by automatic writing: the entranced hand as the deity's instrument, in Nao's kana torrent and the beaked basket of the Cao Đài séances.
The Hộ Pháp is by office the head of the Palace Uniting Heaven and Earth, the branch through which his own mediumship was constitutionally certified.
Phạm Công Tắc was the chief hand at the beaked-basket planchette that produced the founding canon.
Ngô Văn Chiêu's 1921 vision on Phú Quốc is the direct origin the Divine Eye's own provenance already cites.
The compiled scripture is the recorded voice of the Supreme Being's own 1926 séance revelations.
The compiled scripture is the direct textual product of the beaked-basket planchette sessions.
The earthly executive palace and the invisible legislative palace are the two halves of the same constitutional design, explicitly paired in the Pháp Chánh Truyền.
Completes the three-branch constitution: legislative (Bát Quái Đài), executive (Cửu Trùng Đài), and the mediumistic-judicial hinge between them (Hiệp Thiên Đài).
The mediumistic-judicial branch and the clerical executive branch, the two halves of visible church governance.
Two of the Tam Trấn, the Three Governors of the Third Amnesty, sharing constitutional office.
The third of the Tam Trấn, completing the trio with the Taoist Giáo Tông Lý Thái Bạch.
the identical bodhisattva imported directly into Caodaism's pantheon under her own name and iconography.
the same apotheosized general worshipped under the same title, absorbed whole from Chinese folk religion into the Caodaist constitution.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a modern new religious movement's literal organ/emblem of divine sight into a higher order of reality behind the visible world.

Cao Đài's Supreme Being is formally addressed as Ngọc Hoàng Thượng Đế — the Jade Emperor's own Vietnamese title — an explicit doctrinal incorporation of religious Taoism's celestial sovereign as the one God of a new universal faith.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly adapts the Daoist Queen Mother of Kunlun's Golden Mother of the Jade Pool (Yaochi Jinmu) into Diêu Trì Kim Mẫu, the supreme Yin principle birthing all spiritual bodies — the corpus's own provenance names the borrowing directly.

dossier.essence: A Vietnamese syncretic monotheism founded in 1926 in Tây Ninh, French Cochinchina, following Ngô Văn Chiêu's 1921 vision of the Divine Eye and séance revelations to a circle of Vietnamese functionaries and notables led by Lê Văn Trung and Phạm Công Tắc. Its full name, Đại Đạo Tam Kỳ Phổ Độ ('Great Way of the Third Universal Salvation'), states the central claim: the one God behind all prior religions has returned to unite them in a final dispensation. Adherents number on the order of a few million, mostly in southern Vietnam, with diaspora congregations in France, the United States, and Australia.
dossier.cosmos: Cao Đài ('Highest Tower'), identified in the revelations with the Jade Emperor and with the God of the Abrahamic faiths, governs through a celestial constitutional order: the invisible Bát Quái Đài, a divine legislature of buddhas, immortals, and saints, issues law transmitted to earth through mediums. History unfolds as three amnesties of revelation — Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, and Christianity belonging to the first two — with the present, third amnesty delivered directly by God rather than through regional founders. Souls transmigrate under karma, ascending a hierarchy of perfection toward reunion with the Supreme Being, while the Holy Mother (Diêu Trì Kim Mẫu) presides over the birth of souls.
dossier.practice: Adherents keep four daily prayer sessions (6 a.m., noon, 6 p.m., midnight) before an altar bearing the Divine Eye, observe graded vegetarianism (ten days per month for the committed, full vegetarianism at higher ranks), and follow Confucian-inflected precepts. A nine-graded clergy modeled partly on Catholic hierarchy — pope (Giáo Tông), censor-cardinals, archbishops, down through student priests, with women eligible up to cardinal rank — administers the religion from the Tây Ninh Holy See. Spirit-writing séances using the cơ bút basket-planchette generated the canon and clerical appointments; official séances have been suspended since the 1975 communist takeover.
dossier.canon: The 'Thánh Ngôn Hiệp Tuyển' (Compilation of Divine Messages), the 'Pháp Chánh Truyền' (Religious Constitution, 1926), and the 'Tân Luật' (New Canonical Code) — all received by séance in the founding years.
dossier.reflects: Syncretism made institutional — the unification of all religions pursued not as private mystical insight but as an administrative, constitutional project with God as head of state.
sources: Victor L. Oliver, 'Caodai Spiritism: A Study of Religion in Vietnamese Society' (1976)
Janet Hoskins, 'The Divine Eye and the Diaspora: Vietnamese Syncretism Becomes Transpacific Caodaism' (2015)
Jérémy Jammes, 'Les Oracles du Cao Đài' (2014)
R. B. Smith, 'An Introduction to Caodaism' (1970)
